title: Today's AI Brief: The Download
summary: Unlocking lithium, controlling Ebola, and a chip startup's bet on AI memory

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to Today's AI Brief. Let's dive into some interesting developments in the world of technology. A new method for extracting lithium could be a game-changer for the industry. According to a report from MIT Technology Review, researchers have discovered a more efficient way to extract lithium, which could help meet the growing demand for this key component in batteries.

Lithium is a critical component in many batteries, and the current extraction process can be costly and inefficient. This new method could help reduce costs and increase supply, which is great news for the electric vehicle industry and renewable energy storage. I am looking at this development as a potential platform shift in the battery industry.

In other news, researchers are using AI to develop new treatments for Ebola. This is an interesting application of AI in healthcare, and it's amazing to see how technology can be used to tackle some of the world's most pressing health challenges.

A chip startup, Xcena, just raised $135 million to develop new memory technology for AI applications. The idea here is that AI's biggest bottleneck isn't compute power, but rather memory. My read is that this could be an interesting approach to addressing some of the current limitations in AI systems.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
